skill.name: ena-database
skill.description: Access the European Nucleotide Archive (ENA) via REST APIs and FTP/Aspera to search and retrieve sequences, raw reads (FASTQ), assemblies, and metadata when you have accession IDs or need metadata-driven discovery for genomics pipelines.
skill.summary: Search, retrieve, and download genomic sequences and metadata from the European Nucleotide Archive using REST APIs, FTP, and Aspera protocols.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 316 potential issues across 3 documentation files. All findings are false positives: backtick patterns are markdown code formatting (not shell execution), network references target legitimate EBI/ENA infrastructure (ebi.ac.uk), SAM references are ENA sample accession prefixes (not Windows credentials), MD5 references are file checksum metadata fields, and FTP references point to ENA's public data download service. This skill is a documentation and reference guide for the European Nucleotide Archive with no executable credentials or data exfiltration. It passed all prior security audits with a Production Ready grade.

medium_findings.title: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 217 instances of backtick usage as Ruby/shell backtick execution. All instances are markdown formatting: inline code delimiters and code fence markers in documentation files. No executable shell injection exists.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All backtick occurrences are within markdown documentation files using standard markdown code formatting syntax. No Ruby files or shell scripts exist in this skill.

low_findings.title: MD5 references as weak cryptographic algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged MD5 usage as weak cryptography. In context, MD5 is used only as file checksum metadata field names (fastq_md5, fastq_bytes, fastq_md5) and as a CRAM reference registry lookup endpoint. These are ENA metadata field identifiers, not cryptographic implementations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: MD5 is used in standard ENA metadata field naming conventions for file integrity verification, which is standard practice in bioinformatics data management.

low_findings.title: SAM references misidentified as Windows SAM database
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged SAM references as Windows Security Account Manager credentials. ENA uses SAM as a prefix for sample accessions (SAMN, SAMEA, SAMD). These are legitimate ENA sample identifiers, not Windows system files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Accession IDs like SAMN01, SAMEA123456 follow ENA's standard sample accession naming convention, documented in their public API. No relation to Windows SAM database.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs to legitimate research infrastructure
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged hardcoded URLs and HTTP library usage. All URLs point to ebi.ac.uk (European Bioinformatics Institute) which is ENA's official public API infrastructure. Network requests are to public endpoints that require no authentication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All network references point to official ENA API endpoints at ebi.ac.uk for public genomic data retrieval. No authentication or credentials are required or documented.

low_findings.title: C2 keyword false positive on MD5 checksum
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged an MD5 checksum example as C2 keywords. The string is a documentation example hash for the CRAM reference registry MD5 lookup endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged string is a documented MD5 checksum example in a CRAM reference registry API documentation section. No command-and-control patterns exist in this skill.

content.user_title: Search and Retrieve Genomic Data from ENA
content.value_statement: Navigating the European Nucleotide Archive's complex API documentation wastes valuable research time. This skill provides structured guidance for searching metadata, retrieving sequences, and downloading sequencing data from ENA using its REST APIs, FTP, and Aspera protocols.
actual_capabilities: Search ENA Portal API for studies, samples, experiments, runs, and assemblies using metadata filters
Retrieve record metadata in JSON, TSV, XML, and EMBL flat file formats by accession ID
Access taxonomic lineage and rank information through ENA Taxonomy REST API
Guide bulk file downloads via FTP and Aspera for large sequencing datasets
Generate reproducible query patterns with exponential backoff for rate limit compliance
Provide cross-reference lookups between ENA entries and external databases
limitations: Cannot execute FTP or Aspera downloads directly; provides download instructions only
Does not support write operations or data submission to ENA
Large result sets require pagination; extremely broad queries may time out
Taxonomy lookups depend on ENA's taxonomy service which may not mirror NCBI Taxonomy exactly

use_cases.title: Retrieve FASTQ Files for a Sequencing Project
use_cases.description: A bioinformatics researcher has a study accession (e.g., PRJEB1234) and needs to download all raw sequencing reads for their analysis pipeline. This skill guides searching Portal API for run metadata, extracting FTP URLs from file reports, and downloading FASTQ files.
use_cases.target_user: Bioinformatics researcher with an ENA study accession

use_cases.title: Discover Samples by Metadata Filters
use_cases.description: A microbiologist wants to find all E. coli samples collected in Europe between 2020 and 2023 with specific sequencing platform requirements. This skill structures complex queries using tax_tree, date ranges, and field filtering to retrieve matching sample records.

use_cases.title: Build a Reproducible Genomics Pipeline
use_cases.description: A computational biologist is building a pipeline that requires consistent metadata retrieval from ENA. This skill provides documented API patterns with rate limiting, error handling, and pagination guidance for reliable automated data fetching.
use_cases.target_user: Computational biologist automating data ingestion workflows

prompt_templates.title: Search for Runs by Study Accession
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to find all sequencing runs for study accession PRJEB1234 in the European Nucleotide Archive. Use the Portal API search endpoint at https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/portal/api/search with result type 'read_run', query 'study_accession=PRJEB1234', and format 'json'. Include fields: run_accession, sample_accession, experiment_accession, tax_id, scientific_name, fastq_ftp. Apply exponential backoff if rate limited.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with a known study accession

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve Taxonomic Lineage for a Sample
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a taxonomy ID 9606 (human) and need its full lineage information from ENA. Use the Taxonomy REST API at https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/taxonomy/rest/tax-id/{tax_id} to retrieve scientificName, rank, and lineage fields. The response is JSON.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding taxonomic context to genomic data

prompt_templates.title: Get File Download URLs for a Sequencing Run
prompt_templates.prompt: I have run accession ERR123456 and need to download its FASTQ files. Query the ENA Portal API filereport endpoint at https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/portal/api/filereport with accession ERR123456, result 'read_run', format 'json'. Request fields: run_accession, fastq_ftp, fastq_md5, fastq_bytes. Parse the FTP URLs from the response.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Multi-Object Metadata Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Find all RNA-Seq samples related to tax_tree(562) (Escherichia coli and descendants) submitted after 2022. Use the ENA Portal API to first search 'read_experiment' with library_strategy=RNA-Seq, then extract sample_accession values. For each sample, retrieve metadata using the Browser API XML endpoint. Apply rate limiting with 0.02s delay between requests.
prompt_templates.scenario: Complex multi-step data discovery across ENA object types

output_examples.input: Search ENA for human (tax_id 9606) RNA-Seq run metadata with FASTQ file locations
output_examples.output: I will search the ENA Portal API for read_run records matching tax_eq(9606) and RNA-Seq experiments. The response will include run_accession, sample_accession, fastq_ftp locations, and study references. Results can be exported as JSON or TSV for pipeline integration. Apply pagination for large result sets to avoid timeouts.

output_examples.input: What is the taxonomic lineage of tax_id 83333 (E. coli K-12)?
output_examples.output: I will query the ENA Taxonomy REST API at /tax-id/83333. The response includes scientificName (Escherichia coli), rank (strain), and full lineage from root through cellular organisms, Bacteria, Proteobacteria, Gammaproteobacteria, Enterobacterales, Enterobacteriaceae, Escherichia coli.

best_practices: Use the Portal API fields parameter to request only needed columns. This reduces response size and improves query performance for large result sets.
Implement exponential backoff with at least 5 retries when receiving HTTP 429 responses. ENA rate limits at 50 requests per second for API endpoints.
Use FTP or Aspera for bulk file downloads instead of HTTP streaming for large FASTQ files. This provides better throughput and reliability for multi-gigabyte datasets.
anti_patterns: Do not loop over individual accessions to fetch metadata one at a time. Use the Portal API with the fields parameter and batch queries to consolidate requests.
Do not use the Browser API for metadata discovery at scale. It retrieves one record at a time and returns verbose XML. Portal API with JSON format is more efficient for bulk operations.
Do not skip rate limit compliance. Sending requests faster than 50 per second causes HTTP 429 errors and data loss. Always include backoff logic in automated pipelines.

faq.question: What is the difference between the Portal API and Browser API?
faq.answer: Portal API (ena/portal/api/search) supports advanced searching with filters and returns JSON, TSV, or CSV for bulk metadata export. Browser API (ena/browser/api/xml/{accession}) retrieves one record at a time by accession and returns detailed XML.

faq.question: Do I need authentication to access ENA data?
faq.answer: No. ENA data is publicly accessible without authentication for searching and retrieving data from all API endpoints. No API keys or login credentials are required for data access.

faq.question: How do I download FASTQ files using this skill?
faq.answer: First use the Portal API filereport endpoint with your run accession to get fastq_ftp URLs. Then use FTP or Aspera clients to download the files. For bulk downloads, enaBrowserTools command-line tools are recommended.

faq.question: What is the rate limit for ENA APIs?
faq.answer: ENA APIs enforce a limit of 50 requests per second. Exceeding this returns HTTP 429. Implement exponential backoff starting from 1 second delay and doubling on each retry.

faq.question: Can I submit new data to ENA with this skill?
faq.answer: No. This skill only covers data retrieval and discovery. Data submission to ENA requires Webin submission tools and a registered account, which are outside this skill's scope.

faq.question: What output formats does the Portal API support?
faq.answer: The Portal API returns JSON, TSV, CSV, and XML formats. JSON is recommended for programmatic processing, TSV for spreadsheet import, and XML for compliance with XML-based pipelines.
